phenomenon: Stellar nucleosynthesis: silicon and oxygen in sand-forming minerals
key_measurement: Silicon burning in massive stars (>8 M☉) occurs at T ~3×10⁹ K (~0.26 MeV) over ~1 day; Si-28 and O-16 solar abundances 7.10×10⁵ and 1.41×10⁷ relative to H=10¹² (Lodders, 2003, ApJ 591)
institutions: California Institute of Technology (Caltech) — W.K. Kellogg Radiation Laboratory
Max Planck Institute for Astrophysics (MPA Garching)
University of Chicago Department of Astronomy and Astrophysics

sources.citation: Burbidge, E.M., Burbidge, G.R., Fowler, W.A., Hoyle, F. (1957) 'Synthesis of the Elements in Stars', Reviews of Modern Physics 29(4):547–650
Lodders, K. (2003) 'Solar System Abundances and Condensation Temperatures of the Elements', Astrophysical Journal 591(2):1220–1247
Arnett, W.D. (1996) 'Supernovae and Nucleosynthesis: An Investigation of the History of Matter, from the Big Bang to the Present', Princeton University Press

visualization_compliance: Onion-shell burning layer order must be accurate from outside in: H → He → C → Ne → O → Si → Fe core — do not invert or omit layers
Silicon burning timescale ~1 day must be indicated if burn-sequence animation is used — do not conflate with oxygen burning (~6 months)
Nuclear binding energy curve (iron peak) must peak at Fe-56/Ni-56, not at lighter elements
Isotope abundances in visualized ejecta must be consistent with Lodders (2003) solar values — label SOLAR PHOTOSPHERE VALUES
Stellar mass scale on any HR diagram must be anchored to observed main-sequence data
Label NOT TO SCALE on cross-sectional stellar interior diagrams; stellar radius scale bar in R☉ required
ILLUSTRATIVE label required on any artist rendering of supernova ejecta — do not present as simulation output unless citing a specific hydrodynamics code run
